content:
# Philippians Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHP_003_page_001.jpg

---

**1** Finally, my brothers, rejoice in the Lord. To write the same things to you is not troublesome for me, and for you it is a safeguard.

**2** Beware of the dogs, beware of the evil workers, beware of the mutilation.

**3** For we are the circumcision, who worship by the Spirit of God and boast in Christ Jesus and place no confidence in the flesh —

**4** though I myself have reason for confidence even in the flesh. If anyone else thinks to have confidence in the flesh, I more so:

**5** circumcised on the eighth day, of the nation of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews; according to the law, a Pharisee;

**6** according to zeal, persecuting the church; according to the righteousness that is in the law, having become blameless.

**7** But whatever things were gain to me, these I have counted as loss on account of Christ.

**8** Indeed, I even count all things to be loss on account of the surpassing worth of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord, on account of whom I suffered the loss of all things, and I count them as refuse, that I may gain Christ

**9** and be found in him, not having my own righteousness which is from the law, but that which is through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God that rests upon faith,

**10** that I may know him and the power of his resurrection and the fellowship of his sufferings, being conformed to his death,

**11** if somehow I may attain to the resurrection from the dead.

**12** Not that I have already obtained this or have already been made perfect, but I press on, that I may also lay hold of that for which I was also laid hold of by Christ Jesus.

**13** Brothers, I do not consider myself to have laid hold of it. But one thing I do: forgetting the things that are behind and straining forward to the things that are ahead,

**14** I press on toward the goal for the prize of the upward calling of God in Christ Jesus.

**15** Therefore, as many of us as are mature, let us think this way; and if in anything you think differently, God will reveal this also to you.

**16** Nevertheless, to what we have attained, let us walk by the same standard.

**17** Become fellow imitators of me, brothers, and observe those who walk according to the pattern you have in us.

**18** For many walk, of whom I told you often, and now tell you even weeping, as enemies of the cross of Christ,

**19** whose end is destruction, whose god is their belly, and whose glory is in their shame — who set their minds on earthly things.

**20** For our citizenship is in heaven, from which also we eagerly await a Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ,

**21** who will transform the body of our humiliation into conformity with the body of his glory, according to the working of his power to subject all things to himself.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

This chapter contains one of Paul's most intensely personal passages in the entire Pauline corpus. All three translators faithfully rendered the three major movements: Paul's renunciation of his Jewish credentials (vv. 1-6), the surpassing worth of knowing Christ (vv. 7-11), and the forward-pressing eschatological life (vv. 12-21).

- **The rhetorical triple warning (v. 2):** All three translators preserved the anaphoric repetition of BLEPETE ("beware/look out/watch out") and the sharp KATATOMHN/PERITOMH wordplay between vv. 2-3. EMET and LOGOS retained "mutilation"; PNEUMA expanded to "those who mutilate the flesh." The consensus preserves the terse noun form.

- **The accounting metaphor (vv. 7-8):** All three recognized Paul's commercial vocabulary — KERDH (gains), ZHMIA (loss), SKUBALA (refuse/garbage). EMET and LOGOS rendered SKUBALA as "refuse"; PNEUMA chose "garbage." The consensus follows LOGOS's "refuse," which preserves the visceral force in formal register. Credit to PNEUMA for capturing the ledger metaphor with "written off."

- **The athletic imagery (vv. 12-14):** All three captured Paul's race metaphor — pressing on, straining forward, the goal and prize. PNEUMA's "finish line" for SKOPON was vivid but interpretive; LOGOS's "goal" was adopted as more faithful to the Greek. Credit to EMET for preserving the passive KATELHFQHN ("I was laid hold of") which all three rendered well.

- **Heavenly citizenship (v. 20):** All three translators rightly emphasized POLITEUMA ("citizenship") and its political resonance in the Roman colony of Philippi. LOGOS and PNEUMA both noted this in their commentary. The consensus follows LOGOS's rendering closely.

- **The Sinaiticus reading at v. 9:** All three translators noted the distinctive EPI THI PISTEI ("upon faith") reading in Sinaiticus, distinguished from the earlier DIA PISTEWS ("through faith") in the same verse. The consensus renders this as "rests upon faith" to capture the dative-locative force of EPI.

- **The shorter reading at v. 16:** All three followed the shorter Sinaiticus text (TW AUTW STOICHEIN) rather than the expanded Byzantine reading. Credit to EMET and LOGOS for noting this textual variant explicitly.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
